**Groen** ([[ɣrun]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Dutch "Help:IPA/Dutch") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:Nl-Groen.ogg "File:Nl-Groen.ogg"); lit. 'Green'), founded as **Agalev**, is a [green](/wiki/Green_politics "Green politics")[[2]](#cite_note-Nordsieck-2)[[4]](#cite_note-4) [Flemish](/wiki/Flemish_Community "Flemish Community") [political party in Belgium](/wiki/Political_parties_in_Belgium "Political parties in Belgium"). The main pillars of the party are social justice, human rights, and ecologism.[[5]](#cite_note-GreenVRT-5) Its French-speaking equivalent is [Ecolo](/wiki/Ecolo "Ecolo"); the two parties maintain close relations with each other.

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Groen_(political_party)&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

### Agalev

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Groen_(political_party)&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Agalev")]

The party's origins can be traced to the early 1970s, specifically the village of [Viersel](/wiki/Viersel "Viersel") near [Antwerp](/wiki/Antwerp "Antwerp"), where [Luc Versteylen](https://nl.wikipedia.org/wiki/Luc_Versteylen "nl:Luc Versteylen"), a [Jesuit](/wiki/Jesuits "Jesuits") priest, led a youth group. Through this he came to hear about the stress children were suffering from due to an increasingly competitive [education system](/wiki/Education_in_Belgium "Education in Belgium"). From this, he and his co-thinkers developed a critique of [consumerism](/wiki/Consumerism "Consumerism") inspired by the values of [early Christianity](/wiki/Early_Christianity "Early Christianity") as an alternative to the modern Catholic church, rejecting competition and consumption in favour of peace, frugality and community. This critique became central to Anders Gaan Leven (meaning "to go and live differently", a Flemish environmental movement. The movement then established Agalev, a working group formed to contest elections, which eventually became Groen.[[6]](#cite_note-6)[[7]](#cite_note-kamer20151029-7)

Agalev first stood candidates in the 1976 local elections in [Antwerp Province](/wiki/Antwerp_Province "Antwerp Province"), before running across Flanders in the [1978 general election](/wiki/1978_Belgian_general_election "1978 Belgian general election"), scoring 0.1 percent of all votes in Belgium. The party's popularity gradually grew, taking 2.33 percent of the Flemish vote in the [first European Parliament elections the following year](/wiki/1979_European_Parliament_election_in_Belgium "1979 European Parliament election in Belgium"), before making a breakthrough in the [1981 federal election](/wiki/1981_Belgian_general_election "1981 Belgian general election"), where they first entered Parliament with two Representatives and a Senator. In the simultaneous provincial elections they took seven seats, with four in Antwerp, two in [East Flanders](/wiki/East_Flanders "East Flanders") and one in [West Flanders](/wiki/West_Flanders "West Flanders"). Following this, Agalev officially became a separate entity from Anders Gaan Leven in March 1982, though the two organisations maintained close ties. That year the party won 44 seats in 64 communes in municipal elections, electing 28 councillors in Antwerp, seven apiece in East Flanders and the [Province of Brabant](/wiki/Province_of_Brabant "Province of Brabant"), and two in West Flanders and obtaining an average of 5.6 percent of the vote where it stood. The party also co-operated with other ecologists to elect another 12 Agalev endorsed councillors. In the [1984 European elections](/wiki/1984_European_Parliament_election_in_Belgium "1984 European Parliament election in Belgium") [Paul Staes](/wiki/Paul_Staes "Paul Staes") was elected with 7.08 percent of the vote in Flanders and 9.25 percent of the vote in Antwerp Province. In the [1985 Belgian general election](/wiki/1985_Belgian_general_election "1985 Belgian general election") scored 6.3 percent of the Flemish vote, electing four Representatives and three Senators.[[8]](#cite_note-8)

In the [1987 Belgian general election](/wiki/1987_Belgian_general_election "1987 Belgian general election"), the party won 4.5 percent and 4.9 percent of Belgian votes to the Chamber of Representatives and Senate respectively, electing six members of the former and five of the latter.[[9]](#cite_note-9) During the eighties, the party was known for being [against nuclear weapons](/wiki/Anti-nuclear_movement "Anti-nuclear movement"), and for being pro-Europe.[[5]](#cite_note-GreenVRT-5)[[10]](#cite_note-10) According to [Sara Parkin](/wiki/Sara_Parkin "Sara Parkin"), much credit for Agalev's early steady progress and absence of internal conflict has been given to the party's political secretary [Leo Cox](https://nl.wikipedia.org/wiki/Leo_Cox_(politicus) "nl:Leo Cox (politicus)"), who was able to balance the demands of different factions within the party whilst also being able to successfully respond to external political developments.[[11]](#cite_note-11)

In 1992, Agalev was asked to support a constitutional change called the [Sint-Michiels agreement](/wiki/Sint-Michiels_agreement "Sint-Michiels agreement"), which would make Belgium a federation. This change required a two-third majority, so the majority needed to convince some parties of the opposition to proceed. Both Agalev and [Ecolo](/wiki/Ecolo "Ecolo") agreed, in exchange for a tax on bottles, the first [ecotax](/wiki/Ecotax "Ecotax") in Belgium. However, after the constitutional change was voted in, the ecotax was cancelled and replaced by a watered-down concept.[[12]](#cite_note-12)[[13]](#cite_note-13)

In the [elections of 1999](/wiki/1999_Belgian_federal_election "1999 Belgian federal election"), Agalev scored 7% federally and 11% regionally (in Flanders). The [Dioxin affair](/wiki/Dioxin_affair "Dioxin affair"), a scandal surrounding [dioxins](/wiki/Dioxins_and_dioxin-like_compounds "Dioxins and dioxin-like compounds") in for-consumption chickens just before the elections, played an important role in the Greens' performance.[[14]](#cite_note-14) The Greens joined the first [Verhofstadt government](/wiki/Verhofstadt_I_Government "Verhofstadt I Government") as part of the "[purple](/wiki/Purple_coalition "Purple coalition")-green" federal coalition from 12 July 1999 until 18 May 2003. Representing Agalev in this federal government, [Magda Aelvoet](/wiki/Magda_Aelvoet "Magda Aelvoet") was Deputy Prime Minister and the Minister of Consumer Affairs, Public Health and the Environment. She tabled legislation on [gay marriage](/wiki/Gay_marriage "Gay marriage"), making Belgium the second country in the world to legalise same-sex marriage.[[15]](#cite_note-15)
Within the same legislature, Magda Aelvoet left her position due to her opposition of a Belgian arms delivery to Nepal, and was succeeded by fellow party member [Jef Tavernier](/wiki/Jef_Tavernier "Jef Tavernier").[[16]](#cite_note-16)

Also following the [elections of 1999](/wiki/1999_Belgian_regional_elections "1999 Belgian regional elections"), the party joined the [Flemish Government](/wiki/Flemish_Government "Flemish Government"), which was composed of the same parties. Agalev supplied two ministers: [Mieke Vogels](/wiki/Mieke_Vogels "Mieke Vogels") became responsible for Wellbeing, Health and Equal opportunities, and [Vera Dua](/wiki/Vera_Dua "Vera Dua") for Agriculture and Environment.

### Renaming to Groen! (2003)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Groen_(political_party)&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Renaming to Groen! (2003)")]

In the federal elections of 2003, Agalev scored less than 5% and lost all their seats (on the federal level). The next day, [Jos Geysels](/wiki/Jos_Geysels "Jos Geysels") resigned as party leader.[[17]](#cite_note-17)[[18]](#cite_note-vrt20240322-18) The sitting ministers in the Flemish government Mieke Vogels and Vera Dua stepped down, and were replaced by [Adelheid Byttebier](/w/index.php?title=Adelheid_Byttebier&action=edit&redlink=1 "Adelheid Byttebier (page does not exist)") [[nl](https://nl.wikipedia.org/wiki/Adelheid_Byttebier "nl:Adelheid Byttebier")] and [Ludo Sannen](/w/index.php?title=Ludo_Sannen&action=edit&redlink=1 "Ludo Sannen (page does not exist)") [[nl](https://nl.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ludo_Sannen "nl:Ludo Sannen")] respectively.

[Vera Dua](/wiki/Vera_Dua "Vera Dua") got elected as chairperson, and on the same day, the party's name was changed to Groen! (*Green!*).[[19]](#cite_note-19) The party got between 5 and 10% of the votes through the elections of the early 00's. They did not participate in a governmental coalition (on any level higher than local).

### Groen (2012–present)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Groen_(political_party)&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Groen (2012–present)")]

In 2012 the party decided to drop de exclamation mark of their name.

After the local and provincial elections of 2014, Groen had a mandate for the first time in the province of [Flemish-Brabant](/wiki/Flemish-Brabant "Flemish-Brabant"). In 2018 the province of [East-Flanders](/wiki/East-Flanders "East-Flanders") followed. The results in Flemish-Brabant had improved though Green wasn't part of the coalition anymore after these elections.

In 2019, there were elections on the regional, federal and European level. At this time the [school strike for climate](/wiki/School_strike_for_climate "School strike for climate") movement had dominated the media, so the party Groen was expected to grow substantially. An total victory didn't happen, though the party gained seats on all levels.[[20]](#cite_note-20)

On the regional level of [Brussels-Capital](/wiki/Brussels-Capital "Brussels-Capital"), Groen participated in a [red-green-blue](/wiki/Politics_and_government_of_the_Brussels-Capital_Region#Cabinet "Politics and government of the Brussels-Capital Region") coalition.[[21]](#cite_note-21) [Elke Van den Brandt](/wiki/Elke_Van_den_Brandt "Elke Van den Brandt") became Minister of Mobility, Public Works, and Road Safety.[[22]](#cite_note-22)

On the federal level, a new government wasn't formed until 17 March 2020, when the [coronavirus outbreak](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic_in_Belgium "COVID-19 pandemic in Belgium") urged a minority government with extra plenary powers to be formed. After 6 months a 'regular' majority government was formed and the [Vivaldi coalition](/wiki/Vivaldi_coalition "Vivaldi coalition") was sworn in with 2 Groen ministers.[[23]](#cite_note-23) [Petra De Sutter](/wiki/Petra_De_Sutter "Petra De Sutter") became Deputy Prime Minister and Minister of Civil Service, Public Enterprises, Telecommunication and Postal Services, as Europe's first transgender deputy prime minister.[[24]](#cite_note-24) [Tinne Van der Straeten](/wiki/Tinne_Van_der_Straeten "Tinne Van der Straeten") holds the Ministry of Energy.[[25]](#cite_note-25)

In 2022 the members of Groen elected new party leaders: the duo [Nadia Naji](/wiki/Nadia_Naji "Nadia Naji") and [Jeremie Vaneeckhout](/wiki/Jeremie_Vaneeckhout "Jeremie Vaneeckhout"), and the logo changed from green only to a more colourful background.[[26]](#cite_note-26)

## Political views

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Groen_(political_party)&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Political views")]

Groen is a [progressive](/wiki/Progressivism "Progressivism") Flemish party that, as the name itself suggests, considers [environmental](/wiki/Environmental_policy "Environmental policy") and [climate policy](/wiki/Politics_of_climate_change "Politics of climate change") very important. The party wants to combine this with attention to [social justice](/wiki/Social_justice "Social justice"), [equal opportunities](/wiki/Equal_opportunities "Equal opportunities"), [human rights](/wiki/Human_rights "Human rights") and [quality of life](/wiki/Quality_of_life "Quality of life"). In other words, the party wants to protect the planet, but wants to do so by paying attention to the weakest in society. The party therefore wants good and affordable [health care](/wiki/Health_care "Health care") and to tackle [poverty](/wiki/Poverty "Poverty"). In order to afford these initiatives, the party expect the richest people and the biggest polluters in society to contribute more.[[5]](#cite_note-GreenVRT-5)
